title: Why 'Deep' Learning is Called Deep
concept: The word "deep" in deep learning literally refers to stacking multiple hidden layers — not just one — and more stacked layers let a network represent more complex patterns.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a neural network, what are the three kinds of layers, in order?

options.text: Input layer, hidden layer(s), output layer
Output layer, input layer, hidden layer
Warmup layer, practice layer, boss layer

exercises.explain: Data flows from the input layer, through hidden layer(s), to the output layer.

cards.title: One floor was just the start
cards.body: Your XOR network had exactly ONE hidden layer. That solved XOR — but what happens if you stack a second hidden layer? A third? That's where the word "deep" comes from.

cards.title: What does "deep" actually mean?
cards.body: Deep in "deep learning" is a structural term: it means a neural network with multiple stacked hidden layers, not just one. The more hidden layers stacked on top of each other, the "deeper" the network.

cards.title: A single-story building vs. a skyscraper
cards.body: A network with one hidden layer is like a single-story building — useful, but limited. A deep network stacks floor after floor, and each new floor builds on whatever the floor below it already figured out.

cards.title: Layers building on layers
cards.body: In a deep network, hidden layer 1's neurons might catch simple patterns in the input. Hidden layer 2 takes THOSE results and combines them into slightly more complex patterns. Hidden layer 3 combines those into even richer patterns — each layer builds on the last.

cards.title: "Deep" isn't about intelligence
cards.body: It's easy to assume "deep" means "deep thinking" or extra-smart. It doesn't — it's purely a count of stacked layers. A network is called "deep" the moment it has more than one hidden layer, regardless of how well it performs.

left.text:
1 hidden layer
Can bend the boundary a little (e.g. solve XOR)

right.text:
Many stacked hidden layers
Can bend the boundary into far more complex shapes

exercises.prompt: What does the word "deep" refer to in "deep learning"?

options.text: A neural network having multiple stacked hidden layers, not just one
How long the network took to train
How large the input numbers are
How many correct answers the network gets

exercises.explain: "Deep" is a structural term describing multiple stacked hidden layers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A neural network with just one hidden layer, like the one that solves XOR, is already considered "deep."
exercises.explain: False — one hidden layer is a "shallow" network. "Deep" means multiple stacked hidden layers.

exercises.sentence: Stacking multiple hidden ___ on top of each other, instead of just one, is what makes a network "deep."
exercises.explain: Depth refers to the number of stacked hidden layers in the network.

exercises.prompt: Which network would correctly be described as "deep"?

options.text: Input layer → one hidden layer → output layer
Input layer → hidden layer → hidden layer → hidden layer → output layer
Input layer → output layer, with no hidden layer at all

exercises.explain: Multiple stacked hidden layers is exactly what makes a network "deep" rather than "shallow."

exercises.prompt: Two networks both correctly solve XOR: Network A has 1 hidden layer, Network B has 4 stacked hidden layers. Which statement is most accurate?

options.text: Network B is "deep" and Network A is "shallow" — depth is about the count of stacked hidden layers, not about which one solves XOR better
Both networks are equally "deep" since they solve the same problem
Network A is deeper because it's simpler and more efficient
Neither network can be called "deep" unless it fails at the task first

exercises.explain: Right! "Deep" vs. "shallow" is purely about how many hidden layers are stacked — Network B, with 4 hidden layers, is deep; Network A, with just 1, is shallow, even though both can solve XOR.
